Janus-QL Specification

Kushagra Singh Bisen (Ghent University - imec)

    1. Normative References

1. Introduction

Janus-QL is a query language for combining live stream windows with historical log windows in a single query.

This specification defines a minimal first public Janus-QL language contract that matches the current validated Janus implementation. It defines only the public query forms that are intended for external users.

This specification describes Janus-QL using:

  • live windows over ON STREAM;

  • historical log windows over ON LOG;

  • WINDOW &lt;name&gt; { ... } blocks inside WHERE; and

  • historical-only nested subqueries inside an outer WHERE.

This specification does not define legacy baseline syntax as part of the public language.

2. Conformance

The key words MUST, MUST NOT, SHOULD, SHOULD NOT, and MAY in this specification are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 [RFC2119] [RFC8174] when, and only when, they appear in all capitals.

A Janus-QL processor conforms to this specification if it accepts every query form defined by this specification and rejects every query form that this specification marks as unsupported or invalid.

This specification is intentionally minimal. A processor MAY implement additional legacy or experimental behavior, but such behavior is non-normative unless this specification explicitly defines it.

3. Query Model

A Janus-QL query supports:

  • zero or more PREFIX declarations;

  • one top-level SELECT clause;

  • zero or more FROM NAMED WINDOW declarations;

  • one WHERE clause;

  • optional top-level GROUP BY and HAVING clauses; and

  • optional REGISTER RStream <output> AS when the query is registered as a live output stream.

A Janus-QL query MUST contain exactly one top-level SELECT clause and exactly one top-level WHERE clause.

A Janus-QL query MAY omit REGISTER RStream <output> AS when the query is executed as a historical-only query.

A query that registers a live output stream SHOULD use REGISTER RStream <output> AS.

A FROM NAMED WINDOW declaration introduces a window name that MAY be referenced later in the WHERE clause by a WINDOW &lt;name&gt; { ... } block.

The public window declaration forms are:

  • FROM NAMED WINDOW &lt;w&gt; ON STREAM &lt;s&gt; [RANGE &lt;range&gt; STEP &lt;step&gt;]

  • FROM NAMED WINDOW &lt;w&gt; ON LOG &lt;l&gt; [START &lt;start&gt; END &lt;end&gt;]

  • FROM NAMED WINDOW &lt;w&gt; ON LOG &lt;l&gt; [OFFSET &lt;offset&gt; RANGE &lt;range&gt; STEP &lt;step&gt;]

4. Window Declarations

Janus-QL defines the following public window declaration forms.

4.1. Live Stream Window {#live-stream-window}

The following form declares a live stream window:

FROM NAMED WINDOW &lt;w&gt; ON STREAM &lt;s&gt; [RANGE &lt;range&gt; STEP &lt;step&gt;]

A processor MUST treat this form as a live window over stream &lt;s&gt;.

4.2. Historical Fixed Log Window {#historical-fixed-log-window}

The following form declares a fixed historical log window:

FROM NAMED WINDOW &lt;w&gt; ON LOG &lt;l&gt; [START &lt;start&gt; END &lt;end&gt;]

A processor MUST treat this form as a historical window over log &lt;l&gt; with explicit start and end bounds.

4.3. Historical Sliding Log Window {#historical-sliding-log-window}

The following form declares a sliding historical log window:

FROM NAMED WINDOW &lt;w&gt; ON LOG &lt;l&gt; [OFFSET &lt;offset&gt; RANGE &lt;range&gt; STEP &lt;step&gt;]

A processor MUST treat this form as a historical window over log &lt;l&gt; that is resolved relative to live evaluation time.

Janus-QL v0.1 documents the current implementation behavior: [T - OFFSET, T - OFFSET + RANGE]. This interpretation should be reviewed before a later stable release. An alternative interpretation, [T - OFFSET - RANGE, T - OFFSET], may be more intuitive because it treats OFFSET as the distance between the live evaluation time and the end of the historical window.

For this form, RANGE MUST NOT be greater than OFFSET. Queries where RANGE > OFFSET MUST be rejected.

5. Window Blocks

A declared window is referenced inside WHERE by a WINDOW &lt;name&gt; { ... } block.

Example:

WINDOW ex:liveWindow {
  ?sensor ex:hasValue ?value .
}

A WINDOW &lt;name&gt; { ... } block MUST refer to a window name declared by a FROM NAMED WINDOW clause in the same query.

The graph pattern inside a WINDOW <name> { ... } block is evaluated against the window selected by that declaration.

6. Historical Subqueries

A nested SELECT block MAY appear inside the outer WHERE clause.

A nested subquery is supported only when every WINDOW referenced inside that nested subquery is a historical ON LOG window.

Live-only nested subqueries are not part of this version of Janus-QL and MUST be rejected.

Mixed live and historical nested subqueries are not part of this version of Janus-QL and MUST be rejected.

Supported historical-only nested subqueries are lowered to a materialized historical result and joined back into the outer query.

A supported historical-only nested subquery MAY reference more than one historical window.

A materialized historical subquery MUST project at least one anchor variable and at least one additional value. A nested historical subquery that projects only one variable MUST be rejected.

7. Evaluation Semantics

Live windows are evaluated relative to the live stream schedule associated with the registered or executing query.

Historical fixed log windows use the explicit [START ... END] bounds given in the corresponding FROM NAMED WINDOW declaration.

Historical sliding log windows are resolved relative to live evaluation time T.

For a declaration of the form:

FROM NAMED WINDOW &lt;w&gt; ON LOG &lt;l&gt; [OFFSET &lt;offset&gt; RANGE &lt;range&gt; STEP &lt;step&gt;]

the current Janus implementation resolves the interval as [T - OFFSET, T - OFFSET + RANGE].

This specification defines that same interval for this version of Janus-QL.

Supported historical-only nested subqueries are evaluated as a historical materialization step whose result is joined back into the outer query during overall query evaluation.

8. Examples

8.1. Live And Historical Query With Nested Historical Subquery {#example-live-historical-nested}

PREFIX ex: &lt;http://example.org/&gt;

REGISTER RStream ex:output AS
SELECT ?sensor ?value ?historicalAverage
FROM NAMED WINDOW ex:liveWindow ON STREAM ex:stream [RANGE 60000 STEP 30000]
FROM NAMED WINDOW ex:historicalWindow ON LOG ex:stream [START 0 END 86400000]
WHERE {
  WINDOW ex:liveWindow {
    ?sensor ex:hasValue ?value .
  }

  {
    SELECT ?sensor (AVG(?oldValue) AS ?historicalAverage)
    WHERE {
      WINDOW ex:historicalWindow {
        ?sensor ex:hasValue ?oldValue .
      }
    }
    GROUP BY ?sensor
  }

  FILTER(?value > ?historicalAverage)
}

8.2. Historical-Only Query Without REGISTER {#example-historical-only-without-register}

PREFIX ex: &lt;http://example.org/&gt;

SELECT ?sensor ?value
FROM NAMED WINDOW ex:historicalWindow ON LOG ex:stream [START 0 END 86400000]
WHERE {
  WINDOW ex:historicalWindow {
    ?sensor ex:hasValue ?value .
  }
}

8.3. Historical Sliding Log Window Declaration {#example-historical-sliding-log-window}

PREFIX ex: &lt;http://example.org/&gt;

REGISTER RStream ex:output AS
SELECT ?sensor ?value
FROM NAMED WINDOW ex:liveWindow ON STREAM ex:stream [RANGE 60000 STEP 30000]
FROM NAMED WINDOW ex:previousHour ON LOG ex:stream [OFFSET 86400000 RANGE 3600000 STEP 30000]
WHERE {
  WINDOW ex:liveWindow {
    ?sensor ex:hasValue ?value .
  }

  WINDOW ex:previousHour {
    ?sensor ex:hasValue ?oldValue .
  }
}

9. Limitations

DEFINE BASELINE and USING BASELINE are not part of the public Janus-QL specification.

The implementation may still contain legacy compatibility paths and internal baseline-related names. Those implementation artifacts are non-normative and do not define the public language.

Older implementation paths may still accept historical [START ... END] and [OFFSET ... RANGE ... STEP ...] forms on ON STREAM. This specification does not define those forms as public Janus-QL syntax. In this version, historical windows are defined only over ON LOG.

Live-only nested subqueries and mixed live/historical nested subqueries are not part of this version of Janus-QL.

This specification does not define unsupported future syntax beyond the public forms listed in this document.

References

Normative References

[RFC2119]
S. Bradner. Key words for use in RFCs to Indicate Requirement Levels. March 1997. Best Current Practice. URL: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc2119
[RFC8174]
B. Leiba. Ambiguity of Uppercase vs Lowercase in RFC 2119 Key Words. May 2017. Best Current Practice. URL: https://www.rfc-editor.org/info/rfc8174/
